questions.q: Razia Sultana was the first and only woman to rule which medieval Indian state?
questions.explanation: Razia was the first and only woman to sit on the throne of the Delhi Sultanate.

questions.q: Razia Sultana was the daughter of which earlier Sultan of Delhi?
questions.explanation: Razia was the daughter of Iltutmish, who chose her as his successor over his sons.

questions.q: Razia ruled the Delhi Sultanate during which years?
questions.explanation: Razia reigned from 1236 to 1240 CE before being overthrown.

questions.q: Razia belonged to which dynasty of the Delhi Sultanate?
questions.explanation: As the daughter of Iltutmish, Razia belonged to the Mamluk or Slave dynasty.

questions.q: From which capital did Razia rule?
questions.explanation: Razia ruled from Delhi, the seat of the Sultanate established firmly under her father Iltutmish.

questions.q: Razia is remembered as a ruler who was:
questions.explanation: Razia is described as able and assertive, taking an active and capable role in governing the Sultanate.

questions.q: Razia faced strong opposition chiefly from which group?
questions.explanation: Razia met resistance from the powerful Turkish nobility who opposed a woman holding supreme power.

questions.q: How did Razia's reign end?
options: She left to become an ascetic
She died of old age on the throne
questions.explanation: Razia was overthrown by the nobility after a reign of about four years.

questions.q: In which century did Razia Sultana rule?
questions.explanation: Razia ruled in the 13th century CE, from 1236 to 1240.

questions.q: Razia's father Iltutmish chose her as heir over his:
questions.explanation: Iltutmish nominated Razia ahead of his sons, judging her more capable of ruling.

questions.q: Razia came to power after the brief reign of which half-brother who proved incompetent?
questions.explanation: Razia replaced her ineffective half-brother Ruknuddin Firuz, whose misrule prompted nobles and people to back her.

questions.q: Razia is said to have appeared in public dressed in which manner, breaking convention?
options: In male attire and riding to lead her army
In the garb of a Sufi ascetic
questions.explanation: Razia discarded the veil, wore tunic and headdress, and rode out to lead her forces, defying convention.

questions.q: Razia's full regnal title was:
questions.explanation: Razia took the title Jalalat-ud-din Raziya and insisted on the masculine 'Sultan' rather than 'Sultana'.

questions.q: Razia preferred the title 'Sultan' rather than 'Sultana' because:
options: It was a foreign Mongol custom
She wished to honour her mother
It was required by the Caliph
'Sultana' then meant merely a sultan's wife
questions.explanation: In that era 'Sultana' denoted a sultan's consort, so Razia insisted on 'Sultan' to assert her sovereign authority.

questions.q: The powerful clique of forty Turkish nobles that opposed Razia is known as the:
questions.explanation: The Chihalgani, the Forty Turkish nobles organised under Iltutmish, resented Razia's independence and conspired against her.

questions.q: Razia provoked noble resentment by favouring which Abyssinian officer with a high post?
questions.explanation: Razia elevated the Abyssinian Jamaluddin Yaqut, which angered the Turkish nobility and fuelled rebellion.

questions.q: Razia's downfall was hastened by a revolt led by the governor of which province?
questions.explanation: Malik Altunia, the governor of Bhatinda, rose against Razia, and his rebellion led to her capture.

questions.q: After her capture by Altunia, Razia reportedly:
options: Married him and tried to regain the throne
questions.explanation: Razia married her captor Altunia and together they marched on Delhi in a bid to recover power.

questions.q: Which half-brother of Razia was placed on the throne by the nobles after her overthrow?
questions.explanation: The nobles raised Razia's half-brother Muizuddin Bahram to the throne after deposing her.

questions.q: Roughly how long did Razia reign over the Sultanate?
options: About three and a half years
questions.explanation: Razia reigned for roughly three and a half years, from 1236 to 1240 CE.

questions.q: Razia is generally praised by chroniclers for which quality despite her short reign?
options: Her justice and capacity to govern
questions.explanation: Contemporary historians such as Minhaj-us-Siraj acknowledged Razia's justice and ability, faulting only that she was a woman.

questions.q: Razia's gender was the principal objection raised against her rule by:
options: The Turkish nobles and orthodox elements
questions.explanation: The chief objection was that a woman ruled supreme, an objection pressed mainly by the entrenched Turkish nobility.

questions.q: Razia and Altunia's final defeat near Delhi led to her death in which year?
questions.explanation: Razia and Altunia were defeated and killed in 1240 CE while attempting to retake Delhi.

questions.q: Which contemporary chronicler, author of the Tabaqat-i-Nasiri, left an account of Razia's reign?
questions.explanation: Minhaj-us-Siraj, writing the Tabaqat-i-Nasiri, was a near-contemporary source for Razia's brief reign.

questions.q: Razia secured the throne in 1236 partly by appealing directly to which group when Ruknuddin's mother schemed against her?
questions.explanation: Razia is said to have appealed to the populace of Delhi, who supported her claim against Ruknuddin's faction.

questions.q: How did Jamaluddin Yaqut, Razia's favoured officer, meet his end?
options: He was killed during the rebellion against Razia
He was made the next Sultan
He fled to Bengal and ruled there
questions.explanation: Yaqut was slain during the noble-led revolt, his prominence having been a chief grievance against Razia.

questions.q: Razia attempted to reduce the dominance of the Turkish nobility by:
options: Promoting non-Turkish officers to high office
questions.explanation: Razia tried to counterbalance the Turkish Forty by raising non-Turks like Yaqut, which deepened noble hostility.

questions.q: Razia's tomb is most traditionally said to be located in which area of old Delhi?
questions.explanation: A modest tomb traditionally identified as Razia's lies in the Bulbul-i-Khana locality near Turkman Gate, though sites are disputed.

questions.q: Razia issued coins in her name bearing which kind of title to assert her sovereignty?
options: Sovereign titles like 'Sultan' and her regnal name
The name of her husband Altunia
questions.explanation: Coins struck in her reign carried her own regnal style, a mark of full sovereignty asserted by Razia.

questions.q: Razia's reign is historically significant chiefly as an example of:
options: A long and stable Sultanate dynasty
A capable woman exercising sovereign rule against entrenched opposition
The expansion of the Sultanate into the Deccan
questions.explanation: Razia stands out as a rare instance of a woman wielding sovereign power, undone less by incapacity than by noble resistance to her gender.
